videos.title: Credit Suisse: The $16 Billion Bond Wipeout Explained
videos.text: Imagine owning bonds, a seemingly safer investment than stocks, only to have $16 billion of them wiped out to zero overnight while shareholders still got something. This wasn't a scam from a fly byite operation. This was Credit Swiss, a 167-year-old titan of Swiss banking. And in March 2023, it evaporated in days. How [music] does an institution built on centuries of trust crumble so spectacularly, so fast? For generations, Credit Swiss was more than just a bank. It was a global symbol of Swiss precision, stability, and wealth management. From its humble beginnings [music] in 1856, funding Switzerland's railways, it grew into [music] a sprawling financial empire, managing trillions for the world's elite. But beneath that polished facade, a rot had begun to set in. Years of scandals and mismanagement slowly eating away at its foundations. The collapse wasn't sudden. It was the explosive culmination of a slow motion catastrophe that had been building for years. For over a century, it thrived, becoming a key player in global finance, a name synonymous with discretion and security. It was the bank where fortunes [music] were made and preserved. Where the world's most powerful individuals and corporations entrusted their wealth. It was a fortress seemingly impregnable. What secret vulnerability lay hidden beneath its polished surface? The seeds [music] of its destruction were sewn not in a single grand fraud, but in a catastrophic [music] breakdown of fundamental banking principles, risk management, due diligence, and ultimately [music] trust. Let's look at two key mechanisms that bled the bank dry. First, [music] imagine a high roller gambler, Bill H. Wang, borrowing massively from multiple casinos, banks like [music] Credit Swiss to place colossal leveraged bets on stocks through his hedge fund, Archgo's capital. Credit Swiss, like a casino blinded by [music] potential profit, kept extending credit, a terrifying blind spot. When H Wang's bets plummeted, the casino [music] discovered it had lent out too much without adequate protection, failing to cut him off quickly enough. The result, a staggering 5.5 billion loss for credit [music] Swiss in a single blow. It was a shocking failure of basic risk control. But this wasn't an [music] isolated incident. Think of Greensil Capital, another partner which specialized [music] in buying invoices, money owed to businesses at a discount, providing immediate cash. Credit [music] Swiss then packaged these into safe investment funds for its clients, promising steady returns. But [music] the reality was far from safe. The model became opaque, heavily exposed to a few struggling companies. When the credit insurance on these underlying loans [music] wasn't renewed, the entire House of Cards collapsed, costing Credit Swiss clients $10 billion. It was like a bank selling safe investment packages filled with IUS from shaky businesses without doing its homework. And these weren't just abstract financial failures. [music] This culture of lacks oversight had real human consequences, even within the bank's own ranks. Take Patricus Lesco, [music] a credit Swiss banker who for years forged client signatures and made unauthorized stock bets causing over $150 [music] million in losses for clients like former Georgian Prime Minister Bzenna Ivani. [music] Leadron was eventually sentenced to prison, but the human toll was immense, ending tragically with his suicide in 2020. This wasn't a lone wolf. [music] It was a symptom of a systemic problem. A bank where internal controls were alarmingly weak. Where misconduct could fester unseen. But these [music] financial wounds and internal scandals were just the beginning of a relentless barrage. The alarm bells had been ringing. [music] In February 2020, CEO Tigen Theum resigned after a corporate [music] espionage scandal. Caught hiring private detectives to track outgoing executives. A deeply problematic internal [music] culture laid bare. Then came the Swiss secrets leak in February 2022, revealing [music] over 30,000 client accounts linked to autocrats, oligarchs, [music] war criminals, human traffickers, and drug dealers. It wasn't just mismanagement. It was a long history of questionable client relationships [music] that shocked the world. And in June 2022, a Swiss court found credit [music] Swiss guilty of failing to prevent money laundering by a Bulgarian cocaine [music] trafficking gang. This wasn't just bad luck. It was a pattern of neglect and [music] complicity. The bank tried to stem the bleeding, appointing Antonio Hortasio as chairman in April 2021 to address risk management, but he too resigned [music] just 9 months later due to breaches of coid9 quarantine rules. The revolving door at the top became a symbol of its instability. By October 2022, Credit Swiss announced a major restructuring, including cutting 9,000 jobs, a desperate attempt to regain control. But the ship was already taking on too much water with clients pulling out a staggering CHF 110.5 billion in the fourth quarter of 2022 alone. The Grand Old Bank was hemorrhaging cash, and the world was watching. But what would be the final blow? The final rapid descent began in March 2023. On February 9th, the bank reported a fullear net loss of CHF 7.3 billion for 2022, its worst since the 2008 financial crisis. Then on March 8th, the collapse [music] of Silicon Valley Bank in the US sent jitters across the global banking sector. The timing couldn't have been worse for Credit Swiss. Just days later on March 14th, the bank delayed its annual report disclosing material weaknesses in its financial reporting processes. For both [music] 2021 and 2022, this wasn't just an accounting error. It was a confession that the bank couldn't even properly track its own books. Then came the fatal blow. On March 15th, the Saudi [music] National Bank, Credit Swiss's largest investor, publicly stated it could not provide any further financial assistance due to regulatory [music] constraints. This wasn't just a vote of no confidence. It was a death nail. Shares plummeted and clients [music] stampeded for the exits with reports of roughly $10 billion in outflows [music] asterisk daily in the week leading to the takeover. The Swiss government, realizing the unthinkable was happening. A global financial meltdown [music] triggered by one of its own venerable institutions, stepped in [music] over a frantic, sleepless weekend described as a shotgun wedding. They orchestrated an emergency takeover by its rival UBS. But at what cost? On March 19th, 2023, [music] the deal was done. UBS acquired credit Swiss for a mere CHF3 billion in an all stock deal. To put that in [music] perspective, it was just 1% of credit Swiss's peak value in 2007. A [music] 167-year-old empire reduced to a whisper. But the human consequences were staggering. For the thousands of employees, the promised 9,000 job cuts were just the beginning with more expected from UPS integration. shareholders, including the Saudi National Bank, [music] saw their investments decimated with the Saudi bank losing almost [music] 80% of its initial investment. But perhaps the most controversial and devastating blow fell upon the additional tier 1 or AT1 bond holders. [music] These are investments designed to absorb losses asterisk before asterisk shareholders. Yet in an unprecedented move, CHF16 billion, that's $17.2 billion of these AT1 bonds were written down to zero, completely wiped out while shareholders still receive some value. [music] This reversal of financial hierarchy sparked outrage and numerous [music] legal challenges with a Swiss court even questioning the legality [music] of the right. It was a stark reminder that in a crisis, even the most established rules can be broken, leaving investors with [music] nothing but a void. And beyond the immediate financial carnage, the [music] Swiss secrets were not the only dark corners of Credit Swiss's past. >> [music] >> Investigation revealed extensive evidence of previously undisclosed Nazil linked accounts at credit [music] suites and its predecessor banks with the bank accused of a pattern of obstruction in past inquiries. [music] This wasn't just a financial collapse. It was the unraveling of a legacy exposing deep ethical failings that stretch back [music] decades. The Swiss economy and its reputation as a stable financial hub took a significant blow. forced to provide over CHF [music] 100 billion in liquidity and initially a CHF9 billion guarantee to smooth the transition. How could a bank fall so far? The collapse of credit Swiss wasn't a random act of fate. It was the [music] predictable outcome of years of unchecked greed, egregious mismanagement, a profound erosion of trust, and a [music] series of scandals that would have sunk a lesser institution long ago. It stands as a chilling testament to how quickly even the oldest and most respected financial giants can fall [music] when their core values are compromised. What does this mean for the global financial system? It's a stark warning. No institution is too big, too old, or too safe to fail. The echoes of Credit Swiss's demise will [music] reverberate through the financial world for years, reminding us that vigilance and accountability are the only true safeguards [music] against the next collapse. And as we continue to sift through the wreckage, we have to ask who will be

videos.title: The Credit Suisse Collapse: The Shocking Fallout You Need to Know About
videos.text: brace yourselves because what I'm about to tell you will blow your mind Credit Suisse one of the world's largest banks is in deep trouble just days after the collapse of two U.S banks Credit Suisse is facing a magnitude of the disaster that is hard to fathom [Music] since its peak scene years ago in 2007 Credit Suisse has lost a staggering 99 of its share price I mean we're talking about a bank that once held a whopping 1.3 trillion Swiss Francs in deposits and let's not forget that Credit Suisse was not some small obscure Bank it was the second largest Swiss bank right after UBS so what caused credit suisse's downfall where did their economics go wrong and how will this affect the global economy these are important questions that we need answers to especially with the current banking crisis that's affecting not just one but two continents around the world but first stay up to date on the latest economic insights and Analysis by subscribing to our Channel join our community of Savvy investors and business enthusiasts who never miss a beat in the ever-changing world of Finance let's start once a trillion dollar company Credit Suisse is now teetering on the edge of bankruptcy but what exactly went wrong it all boils down to the four streams of Revenue that make up the Bank wealth management Swiss retail banking Investment Banking and asset management these divisions were all doing incredibly well generating billions of dollars in profits each year but there was a dark side lurking beneath the surface Credit Suisse was embroiled in scandal after scandal from money laundering to helping Americans evade taxes to corporate Espionage in 2014 the bank was slapped with a massive 2.6 billion dollar fine and admitted to helping U.S citizens evade taxes over several decades this was followed by another significant settlement in 2017 where the bank paid over 5 billion dollars to settle with U.S authorities over allegations that it misled investors in residential mortgage-backed Securities sold before the 2008 financial crisis in 2019 the bank was hit by a corporate s Espionage Scandal that saw several Executives implicated in ordering private detectives to track two outgoing Executives as a result the bank faced regulatory investigations and its then CEO Mr thiam stepped down from his position in 2020 and despite being fined billions of dollars the bank remained profitable and trusted by its customers however credit suisse's luck ran out when it got involved with two high-profile disasters greensill capital and archegos the collapse of greensill and the ensuing Fallout caused one hedge fund to short Credit Suisse resulting in huge losses and then came archigos which dealt Credit Suisse a staggering 5 billion dollars in losses the largest hit among all the banks involved to put it straight it all started with a risky exposure to the U.S hedge fund archigos Capital Management leading to a whopping 5.5 billion loss in 2021 but that was just the beginning of their woes as a major backer of green silk capital Credit Suisse sold bonds to their High net worth clients touting them as nearly risk-free but in 2021 greensil collapsed forcing Credit Suisse to suspend 10 billion dollars of investor funds this left the rich depositors frustrated causing a massive outflow of almost 100 billion Swiss Francs in just the last quarter of 2022 to make matters worse rumors of the bank shutting down added fuel to the fire as a result Credit Suisse reported negative net income in every quarter of 2022 with a pre-tax loss of 3.3 billion Swiss Francs a five-fold increase from the previous year and just when everyone thought things couldn't get worse the failure of several U.S banks including Silicon Valley Bank shook the market and sent Panic waves across credit suisse's depositors and investors their stock price fell to just 1.8 Swiss Francs a 74 percent decrease from the previous year to add insult to injury their credit default swaps skyrocketed in value this shattered public confidence in the bank and fewer and fewer people wanted to do business the bank then appointed a new CEO by the name of Ulrich Kerner in July 2022 to make sure that more people don't go away he then unveiled a new strategic plan but failed to win over more investors the plan was to raise the capital with gross proceeds of 4 billion Swiss Francs but this further spooked the investors overall their shares went tumbling and they were in for the biggest crunch things weren't looking well and at this backdrop last week their biggest shareholder the chairman of Saudi National Bank announced not to buy any more shares this caused an extreme amount of panic and that too with the news of the fall of Silicon Valley Bank thus the shares of Credit Suisse plugged to around 25 percent in one single day we would like you to subscribe to the channel if you are loving the content now on the 16th of March 2023 the group announced that it is taking decisive action to strengthen its liquidity by exercising its option to borrow from the Swiss National Bank up to 50 billion Swiss Francs this might sound good but the reality is that Credit Suisse has now merged with UBS this merger will see UPS buy Credit Suisse for 3.2 billion dollars which is a worry for the bondholders this acquisition of Credit Suisse by its rival UBS has caused turmoil in the financial sector investors are scrambling to understand what it means for the market but one thing is Clear Credit suisse's decision to write off a large chunk of its additional Tier 1 bonds is causing anger and concern at1 bonds are a type of cocoa Bond designed to hedge against future Financial crises they behave like bonds that offer high yields and good times but when things don't go well they switch to Banks or write them down reducing the bank's expenses and absorbing losses at-1 Bond holders are supposed to outperform stockholders in the order of first payers but the Credit Suisse listing shows that stockholders are getting paid before at-1 bondholders this worried investors who thought they were buying a mix of Securities between equity and debt this could spell trouble for the at1 bond market and some are even saying it could be the end of the asset class this is bad news for banks which need investment but now the biggest question is whether will it lead to a big banking crisis as after the Silicon Valley Bank Credit Suisse is in trouble many experts think that this is not the case Michael Berry has come on Twitter saying that the crisis will end sooner as he is not seeing any big danger Steve Eisman asserted that he doesn't see another Global Financial contagion like in 2008. he said in a CNBC show my partners and I think that large U.S banks are better to capitalize on than the Swiss banks and when you analyze the statement further you come to realize that he's right the banks in America may be facing challenges in terms of interest rates but they don't have large systematic issues secondly central banks across the globe might be seeing these developments really quickly if a crisis emerges they will come to the Forefront and would try to bail out to avoid a bigger crisis another example can be taken from the response of recent Banks the Bank of Canada the bank of England the bank of Japan and the European Union all have pledged to work in coordination to avoid any economic recession there is no denying the fact that one of the world's biggest bank is on its knees and no one except its own negligence is responsible for this mess the merger of Credit Suisse with UBS has also been seen as a worrying sign for bondholders though there's so much hype about the subject the reality is that there are limited chances of a global crisis American banks are more capitalized and Global central banks are better equipped than in 2008 to counter any shocks let's see what the future holds for Credit Suisse if you like our content do subscribe to the channel for more updates and do let me know your thoughts on this banking crisis

videos.title: Additional Tier 1 (AT1) Bonds Explained: The $17 Billion Disappearing Act - Credit Suisse Collapse
videos.text: Welcome back, everyone. Today, we're going to dive into a financial mystery. We're going to talk about a weekend in March 2023 where $17 billion just vanished into thin air. Poof, gone. I'm talking about the spectacular collapse of Credit Suisse, a bank that had been around for over 160 years. But, we aren't just going to talk about the bank failing. We're going to focus on a specific group of investors who woke up on a Monday morning to find out that their seemingly secure investments were suddenly worth exactly zero. At the exact same time, the supposedly riskier stock market investors actually got to walk away with some money. To the financial world, this was like watching gravity work in reverse. It turned everything we know about investing completely upside down. To understand why this happened and why Wall Street completely panicked, we need to understand the weapon at the center of this drama, the additional tier one bond, or as it is sometimes called, the contingent convertible bond. Now, I know contingent convertible bond sounds like a phrase designed to put you to sleep. But, stick with me. Let's start from scratch. Forget about Wall Street for a minute. Let's talk about Main Street. Imagine you want to open a neighborhood bakery. You need money to buy ovens, flour, and a storefront. You essentially have two ways to get this money, debt and equity. First, you can use equity. You put your own life savings and maybe you convince your best friend to invest some money, too. In exchange, you and your friend own the bakery. You hold shares. If the bakery becomes the next big global donut franchise, you both become billionaires. The upside is unlimited. But, if nobody buys your bread and the bakery goes bankrupt, your shares become worthless. You lose everything. That is the risk of equity. High risk, infinite potential reward. The second way to get money is debt. You go to the local bank and ask for a loan or you issue a bond. A bond is simply an IOU. You borrow money from an investor and you promise to pay them back the original amount in 10 years plus a steady fixed interest rate every single year. If you buy a bond, your upside is strictly capped. Even if that bakery makes a billion dollars, you only get your fixed interest. However, your risk is much lower. Why? Because of the golden rule of finance. Debt is senior to equity. If the bakery goes bankrupt, the business has to sell off its ovens and its building to pay people back. The golden rule dictates that the bondholders, the people who lent the money, get paid back first. Only if there's money left over do the owners, the shareholders, get a penny. In the financial world, shareholders are the first ones to take a hit and bondholders are shielded. Equity takes the pain so debt can remain safe. Remember this golden rule because we're going to see it shattered later on. Now, let's scale up. A bank is, in many ways, just a giant financial bakery. But instead of buying flour and selling bread, a bank takes in deposits from everyday people and lends that money out to businesses and home buyers. When a normal bakery goes bankrupt, it is a tragedy for the owner. When a massive global bank goes bankrupt, it is a tragedy for the entire global economy. We all saw this happen in the year 2008 during the global financial crisis. Banks had made terrible loans. When those loans went bad, the banks equity was wiped out instantly. The banks were on the verge of total collapse because millions of everyday people had their life savings in those banks, governments around the world panicked. They realized they could not let these banks fail. So, they used taxpayer money to bail them out. After the dust settled from 2008, global regulators were furious. They gathered together and made a vow, "Never again. Taxpayers will never again pay for the mistakes of billionaire bankers." The regulators told the banks, "You need a thicker shield. You need a way to absorb massive losses without going bankrupt and without asking the government for money." But, banks did not want to issue more standard equity because that dilutes the ownership of the current shareholders. So, the financial engineers went into their laboratories and invented a mutant hybrid, a Frankenstein monster of the financial world. They called it the additional Tier 1 bond. To understand the additional Tier 1 bond, think about the airbag in your car. When you're driving down the highway safely, the airbag just sits there hidden in the steering wheel doing absolutely nothing. But, the second you get into a horrific crash, the airbag violently deploys. It sacrifices itself, destroying the dashboard in the process, to save the life of the driver. An additional Tier 1 bond is a financial airbag for a bank. In normal sunny times, an additional Tier 1 behaves exactly like a normal bond. The investor gives the bank a million dollars and the bank pays the investor a really handsome interest rate every year. In fact, the interest rate on additional Tier 1 bonds is usually very high, often around 7 or 8%, which is incredibly attractive for investors looking for steady income. But, there is a catch, a massive, terrifying catch hidden in the contract. If the bank starts losing money and its capital levels drop below a certain critical danger zone, if the car crashes, a trigger is pulled. Suddenly, the additional Tier 1 bond stops being a bond. The airbag deploys. Depending on the specific contract, one of two things happens. Either the bond is forcibly converted into bank stock, meaning you go from being a safe lender to a risky owner of failing bank. Or even worse, the bond is simply written down to zero. The bank says, "Thank you for the million dollars. We are keeping it forever to save ourselves from bankruptcy." And you get absolutely nothing. The investor loses their entire investment, but the bank survives without needing a taxpayer bailout. The investors accepted this extreme risk in exchange for getting those juicy high interest payments during the good times. Now we arrive at the main character of our story, Credit Suisse. Credit Suisse was not just any bank. It was a titan, a symbol of Swiss financial reliability. But over the last decade, it stumbled from one massive scandal to another. They were involved in corporate spying, massive trading losses, money laundering scandals, and horrible risk management. By late 2022 and early 2023, trust in Credit Suisse was evaporating. And in banking, trust is the only thing that actually matters. If people do not trust a bank, they pull their money out. This leads us to the fateful month of March 2023. Panic was already spreading in the financial system because a few regional banks in the United States had just collapsed. The market looked at Credit Suisse and saw a wounded animal. Customers began pulling their money out of Credit Suisse at an astonishing, terrifying speed. We're talking about billions of dollars leaving the bank every single day. It was a classic bank run accelerated by smartphone banking. By Friday, March 17th, the Swiss government looked at the math and realized a horrifying truth. Credit Suisse was not going to survive the weekend. When the markets open on Monday, the bank would default, sending shockwaves through the entire global financial system. The Swiss government, the central bank, and the regulators locked themselves in a room that weekend. They needed a rescue plan, and they needed it before Monday morning. The regulators decided that the only way to save the system was a shotgun wedding. They were going to force Switzerland's other massive bank, UBS, to buy Credit Suisse and swallow it whole. UBS did not want to do this. Buying a failing bank is like buying a burning house. So, the Swiss government had to offer UBS some massive financial sweeteners and guarantees to make the deal happen. Finally, late on Sunday night, the deal was struck. UBS agreed to buy Credit Suisse for a fraction of what it used to be worth. They bought it for roughly 3.2 billion dollars. This meant that the shareholders of Credit Suisse, the owners, took a massive loss, but they didn't lose everything. They got a few pennies on the dollar. They got that 3.2 billion. But then came the announcement that shook the financial world to its core. The Swiss financial regulator, an agency known as FINMA, stepped up to the microphone. They announced that as part of this emergency rescue deal, all 17 billion dollars worth of Credit Suisse's additional tier one bonds are going to be written down to absolutely zero. Let that sink in. 17 billion dollars of investor money, held by pension funds, mutual funds, and wealthy individuals around the world, was instantly vaporized. The airbag had deployed. When the markets opened on Monday, there was absolute chaos. But the anger was not just because the investors lost money. Investors lose money all the time. The outrage was about who lost money. Let us go back to our bakery analogy and the golden rule of finance. Remember? Debt is always senior to equity. If the ship sinks, the shareholders drown first. The bondholders are supposed to be protected until the very end. But the Swiss regulators flipped the hierarchy completely upside down. The shareholders, the people who actually own the terribly managed bank, walked away with over $3 billion. Meanwhile, the additional tier one bondholders who had lent money to the bank were wiped out completely to zero. Global bond investors screamed foul play. How could the owners get paid while the lenders got nothing? It felt like a violation of the most sacred law of capitalism. Lawyers began sharpening their knives. People threatened to sue the Swiss government. So, how did the Swiss regulators get away with this? How did they justify breaking the golden rule? The answer lies in the deeply buried, excruciatingly boring fine print of the Swiss additional tier one bond contracts. You see, additional tier one bonds issued in different countries have slightly different rules. In most of Europe and the United Kingdom, the contract says that an additional tier one bond cannot be wiped out unless the shareholders are also wiped out first. But the Swiss contracts had a unique, highly specific clause. It was called a viability event clause. This clause essentially stated that if the bank requires exceptional government support to survive, if the government has to step in to stop the bank from going into bankruptcy, the regulator has the absolute legal right to instantly vaporize the additional tier one bonds regardless of what happens to the shareholders. Because the Swiss government had to provide massive financial guarantees to UBS to make the purchase happen, they triggered the viability event. The government argued that without their intervention, Credit Suisse would have gone totally bankrupt on Monday anyway, in which case both the shareholders and the bondholders would have gone to zero. By stepping in, they used the additional tier one money to help plug the financial hole, which allowed UBS to buy the remaining shell of the bank. The investors had signed a contract. They had enjoyed years of high interest payments, but they had ignored the fine print. They assumed that because a bond wipeout usually follows a shareholder wipeout, it always would. The Swiss regulators proved them wrong to the tune of $17 billion. So, what is the moral of this incredible financial story? First, it is a reminder that there is no such thing as a free lunch in investing. If an investment is offering you a surprisingly high interest rate, like those 7 or 8% yields on additional tier one bonds during an era when normal bank accounts paid nothing, there is always a hidden risk attached to it. The market is compensating you for a danger you might not fully understand. Second, it proves that when the global financial system is on the brink of a meltdown over a weekend, governments will do whatever it takes to stop a panic, even if it means bending the traditional rules of the market. They care about saving the economy, not about saving sophisticated bond investors. And finally, and perhaps most importantly, it highlights the absolute necessity of reading the fine print. Thousands of professional investors, people who manage billions of dollars for a living, lost their shirts because they assumed they knew how the airbag worked. They didn't read the manual. The Credit Suisse collapse will be studied in business schools for decades. It is a brilliant, terrifying masterclass in risk, regulation, and the sudden, brutal reality of contingent convertible bonds. Thank you all for tuning in today. If you found this breakdown helpful, make sure to like, subscribe, and share this with anyone trying to navigate the wild world of finance. Keep reading those contracts, stay safe in the markets, and I will see you in the next video.

videos.title: Credit Suisse Crisis explained | What triggered Credit Suisse Collapse? | @Pavansathiraju
videos.text: Hello everybody. In this episode, we are talking about the crisis that's been evolving at Credit Suisse. This is a story of finance gone wrong. The Credit Suisse story is a story of one of Switzerland's most important institutions, one of the most important banking institutions in Europe. They say that all press is positive. That isn't the case for Credit Suisse, which has had more than its share of bad headlines. The shares of the embattled Swiss lender Credit Suisse lost nearly about 20% of their value today. This after the company said that it had found material weakness in its financial reporting between the years 2021 till 2022. Credit Suisse is considered to be a systematically important bank in the world. Which means what we are witnessing. There was news about crisis situation at Credit Suisse, and we witnessed that almost all the stock markets around the world were crashing. Asian markets, American markets, and European markets, all of them were down. Now, the magnitude of the problem with Credit Suisse is much bigger than the problem at Silicon Valley Bank. And just to understand the business model itself for Credit Suisse is way different compared to HDFC Bank or Silicon Valley Bank. In this video, we are going to analyze how Credit Suisse is different compared to other banks. Number two, what are some of the issues that it has been facing, and what are the trigger points for this specific collapse. And number three, what are the takeaways for you and me from this entire crisis situation. This is going to be a very important and interesting video. Please do click on that like button, and also stay with me till the very end. I'm sure you'll have a lot of learnings. Also, consider sharing with your friends. I believe these concepts are important to learn, not just for you and me, but for everybody else out there as well. Now, let's start with the model of Credit Suisse. Credit Suisse in 2022 made 15 billion dollars in revenues, and this 15 billion dollars were coming from three different streams. Number one is their wealth management and asset management streams, which contributed to 40% of the revenues. And the second stream is their investment banking division contributing to 30% of the revenues, and finally, commercial and retail banking, just like any other bank. That was contributing to the remaining 30%. Now, the problem areas for Credit Suisse are in the wealth management, asset management, and the investment banking. So, let's deep dive and understand what is happening with the wealth management space. So, to start off with, what is wealth management? If you and I go to HDFC and deposit our money, we get an interest. Let's say 5%. So, on 10,000 it is 500 rupees, and we take it. And subsequently, the bank will then give out that 10,000 rupees as loans to different other people in need, and they charge probably 10%. That difference between 10% the loan interest rate and the 5% interest rate that bank is paying to you and me, that is the differential interest income which the bank is making. Now, in the case of wealth management, it works differently. The people who are their customers are rich people to start with. And these rich people do not go in and deposit 10,000 rupees. They rather deposit hundreds of crores. Let's imagine Bill Gates depositing 1,000 crores. And to manage that 1,000 crores, Credit Suisse will say to Bill Gates, "Hey, I'm managing your money, so I'll take 1% as maintenance fees." So, out of those 1,000 crores, it will take 10 crores as fees. And then subsequently, it will strategize with Bill Gates and take that 1,000 crores and either buy stocks or buy bonds or do something else in the market in collaboration with or in understanding with Bill Gates. Now, this wealth management goes beyond just investment and it is also about your retirement management, your tax planning, so on and so forth. So, this is a holistic management of wealth for rich people. There are commissions as well. If these stocks give good returns, then of course Credit Suisse will say, "Hey Bill Gates, I'm investing in these companies. You are making a lot of profit, so I'll take a commission." So, commissions, fees, these are some of the primary revenue drivers in the wealth management division. Now, what is the problem for Credit Suisse? For the rich people to come and invest money, they need to make sure that they trust the bank to start with. And the issue with Credit Suisse is that they were involved in a lot of financial scandals. So, this is a bank that's really been struggling to recover from just a string of scandals and losses. So, what An investigation led by German daily the Süddeutsche Zeitung has revealed that Switzerland's second biggest lender knowingly managed hundreds of millions of dollars for suspected war criminals, corrupt autocrats, and drug dealers. That's according to an investigation by a consortium of over 40 media organizations worldwide. And that was reducing the trust among rich people for this specific bank. And that is the reason why in 2022 alone, the start of the year, they were managing 900 billion dollars of money for rich people. And by the end of the year in 2022, they were managing 580 billion dollars. So, there was almost a drop of 300 billion dollars of wealth that they were managing. You can imagine the total amount of fees and commissions revenue they lost. Huge, huge. And that another element which is triggering this kind of outflows is social media. In October 2022, without any specific reason, there was a social media post, viral tweets about Credit Suisse. And the tweets were all about that Credit Suisse is going to go down. And these social media tweets led to almost outflows of 100 billion dollars of the money that they were managing. Now, you can understand trust is the important thing, and social media was driving negative sentiment which was hurting this trust. And at that point in time, there was so much money that was lost and revenues potential that was lost, they had to bring in external funding. And that external funding came from Saudi National Bank.
